content.text: The wind howled through the cracks of the old stone tower, a sound like a thousand whispering voices.
Inside, the fire had long since burned to embers, casting flickering shadows that twisted along the walls like grasping fingers.
The young man, Per, sat hunched over a wooden bowl, his hands trembling as he stirred the thin gruel.
He had been a prince once, or so he had been told, but that life was a distant memory now.
The troll who owned this place had taken everything from him—his name, his freedom, his very will.
The door creaked open, and a figure slipped inside.
She was tall, with hair the color of midnight and eyes that held the cold gleam of starlight.
The Master Maid, they called her, though Per had never dared to speak her name aloud.
She moved silently, her bare feet making no sound on the stone floor, and knelt beside him.
"You should not be here," she murmured, her voice like the rustle of dead leaves.
"If he finds you, he will flay you alive."  Per swallowed hard. "I have to know. What is he?
What does he want?"  The Master Maid’s lips curled, not in a smile, but in something far more terrible.
"He is older than the mountains, older than the forests. He feeds on fear, on suffering.
And he has chosen you to be his next feast."  Per’s breath hitched.
The troll had already made him perform impossible tasks—sorting grains of sand, weaving ropes from spider silk, all under the threat of unspeakable torment.
But this was worse. This was a death without end. That night, the Master Maid returned.
"Come with me," she said, pressing a small knife into his palm.
"There is a way out, but you must be swift.
And silent."  They crept through the tower’s winding passages, past rooms where things writhed and moaned in the dark.
The air was thick with the scent of damp earth and something fouler, like rotting meat.
Per’s heart pounded so loudly he was sure the troll would hear it.
But the Master Maid moved like a shadow, pulling him forward.
At last, they reached a narrow window high above the ground. "Jump," she whispered.
"The river below will carry you away."  Per hesitated. The drop was sheer, the water black and churning.
But the alternative was worse. He climbed onto the sill, took a deep breath, and leapt.
The fall seemed to last forever. The cold water swallowed him whole, dragging him down into its depths.
He fought, kicking wildly, until his head broke the surface. Gasping, he looked back at the tower.
It loomed like a jagged tooth against the storm-wracked sky, its windows glowing with an eerie, sickly light.
And then, from the highest chamber, came a sound—a roar of fury that shook the very earth.
Per did not wait to see more.
He swam for the opposite bank, his limbs numb with cold, his lungs burning.
When he finally dragged himself onto the shore, he collapsed, shivering, his clothes heavy with water.
The forest around him was silent, the trees standing like sentinels in the dark. But he was not alone.
A hand touched his shoulder.
He flinched, expecting the worst, but it was the Master Maid who knelt beside him.
Her skin was no longer pale, her eyes no longer cold.
She was radiant, her hair now a cascade of gold, her dress woven from sunlight itself.
"You are free," she said softly. "And so am I."  Per stared at her, uncomprehending.
"But how—?"  She smiled, and for the first time, it was warm.
"The troll’s power was bound to this place. You broke the spell when you escaped.
And now, so have I."  Behind them, the tower groaned, its stones shifting like living flesh.
A great crack split its side, and with a final, shuddering scream, it collapsed in on itself, crumbling into dust.
The wind carried the last of its ashes away, and the forest sighed in relief.
The Master Maid took Per’s hand. "Come. There is a kingdom waiting for you.
And a throne."  Per looked at her, then at the ruins of the tower, then back at her.
The fear was still there, coiled tight in his chest, but so was something else—hope.
For the first time in years, the future stretched before him, bright and endless.
Together, they walked into the dawn.
